1: homotrópōs — in the same way (connecting to the submission pattern of 2:13-18 — wives' submission is one instance of the general Christian submission pattern). hina kai ei tines apeithousīn tō logō — so that even if some disobey the word (the logos = the gospel word; the husbands in view are unbelievers who have not responded to the preached gospel). aneu logou kerdēthēsontai — they will be won without a word (the irony: the logos they refuse from preaching they may receive through living).
2: epopteusantes tēn en phobō agnēn anastrophēn hymōn — having observed your pure conduct in reverence. Epopteusantes (aorist participle — having observed): the husbands observe; the observation precedes the winning. Phobos (reverence/fear) — as throughout the letter, this is reverence for God rather than fear of the husband; the wife's conduct is directed Godward, not primarily husbandward.
3: hōn estō ouch ho exōthen embroplokēs trichōn — whose beauty should not be the external braiding of hair. The three prohibitions: elaborate hair-plaiting (embroplokē), putting around gold (perithesis chrysiōn), wearing of clothes (endysis imatiōn). The third prohibition (wearing clothes) is obviously not absolute; the pattern (external → internal) shows the prohibition is against prioritizing outward display. Roman elite women's elaborate hair constructions are the specific cultural reference.
4: ho kryptos tēs kardias anthrōpos — the hidden person of the heart (the interior self that is invisible to outside observation but fully known to God). to aprauton kai hēsychion pneuma — the gentle and quiet spirit: praus (gentle, not pushing its own claims) + hēsychos (quiet, not demanding to be heard). ho estin enōpion tou Theou polyteles — which is before God very costly/precious (polyteles = very valuable — the comparative register of God's evaluation vs. human evaluation).
5: hai hagiai gynaikes hai elpizousai eis Theon — the holy women who were hoping into God (present participle — their characteristic posture was active hope toward God, not passive resignation). ekosmoun heautas hypotassomenai tois idiois andrasin — used to adorn themselves submitting to their own husbands (the adornment and the submission are simultaneous — the submission is the adornment).
6: hōs Sarra hypēkousen tō Abraam, kyrion auton kalousa — as Sarah obeyed Abraham, calling him lord (the reference is to Gen 18:12 where Sarah uses adon/lord for Abraham in her internal speech — an act of deep disposition, not public performance). hēs egenēthēte tekna agathopoiousai — whose daughters you have become by doing good (the genealogy is ethical and behavioral — becoming daughters of Sarah by living as she lived, not by biological descent). mē phoboumenai mēdemian ptoan — not fearing any alarm/sudden terror (ptoa = sudden fright from a startling thing).
7: kata gnōsin — according to knowledge (with insight/understanding — not instinctively but intelligently). gynaikeiō skeuei hōs asthenesterō — to the female vessel as the weaker (skeuos = vessel, instrument — the body as the physical container of the person; asthenesteron = comparatively weaker in physical capacity, not morally or spiritually inferior). synklēronomois charitos zōēs — co-heirs of the grace of life (both husband and wife are joint inheritors; the syn- prefix emphasizes the co-equality). eis to mē enkoptesthai tas proseuchas — lest your prayers be hindered/cut off (enkoptō = to cut into, interrupt).
8: Five community virtues in asyndeton: homophrōnes (like-minded — literally one-fronted/of one mind), sympatheis (sympathetic — feeling-with), philadelphoi (brother/sister-loving), eusplagchnoi (tender-heartedly compassionate — literally good-boweled, since the splanchna were the seat of emotion), tapeinophrones (humble-minded — literally low-fronted; not servile but unpretentious in self-assessment). The list moves from cognitive unity to affective engagement to practical disposition.
9: mē apodidontes kakon anti kakou ē loidorian anti loidorias — not repaying evil for evil or insult for insult; apodidōmi (to give back, repay) — the exchange-vocabulary of reciprocity that governs honor culture is negated. tounantion de eulogoūntes — but on the contrary blessing. eis touto gar eklēthēte — for to this you were called: the calling to blessing-as-response (not just blessing as an add-on). Hina eulogian klēronomēsēte — that you might inherit blessing.
10: Psalm 34:12-16 LXX citation runs through vv.10-12. ho thelōn zōēn agapan kai idein hēmeras agathas — the one who desires to love life and see good days (the appetite for life is the assumed motivation). pausatō tēn glōssan apo kakou — let him stop the tongue from evil (pausō — the causative/reflexive use: cause the tongue to stop). The conjunction of tongue-control and life-love connects speech ethics directly to quality of living.
11: ekklīnatō de apo kakou kai poiēsatō agathon — let him turn away from evil and do good (two aorist imperatives from Ps 34 — decisive actions, not ongoing habits). zētēsatō eirēnēn kai diōxatō autēn — let him seek peace and pursue it (diōkō = to pursue as a runner pursues a goal; the aggressive pursuit of peace rather than passive waiting for it). The hunting/racing metaphor applied to peace-seeking reverses the aggressive pursuit of status characteristic of honor culture.
12: hoti ophthalmoi Kyriou epi dikaious — for the eyes of the Lord are on the righteous (the divine surveillance is benevolent — watching to protect and answer). kai ōta autou eis deēsin autōn — and his ears toward their petition (the posture of attentiveness). prosōpon de Kyriou epi poiountas kaka — but the face of the Lord against those doing evil (prosōpon in opposition = the face turned against; contrast with the attentive gaze toward the righteous).
13: kai tis ho kakōsōn hymas — and who is going to harm you? (rhetorical question expecting "no one" — if God's eyes are on the righteous, v.12, who can successfully harm them?) ean tou agathou mimētai gēnēsthe — if you become imitators of the good (mimētai = imitators, those who pattern themselves on a model).
14: all'ei kai paschoite dia dikaiosynēn — but even if you should suffer because of righteousness (fourth-class condition — the optative of remote possibility — not denying the possibility but acknowledging its cost). makarioi — blessed (the beatitude-form; the only explicit beatitude in 1 Peter). ton de phobon autōn mē phobēthēte — their fear do not fear (Isa 8:12 LXX echo; the paronomasia phobos/phobeomai — fear-their-fear is a trap to be avoided).
15: Kyrion de ton Christon hagiasate en tais kardiais hymōn — but sanctify Christ as Lord in your hearts (the textual variant: some manuscripts read Kyrion ton Theon following Isa 8:13 LXX exactly — "sanctify the LORD of hosts"; Peter's adaptation replaces Theon with ton Christon, applying the Isa 8:13 command directly to Christ). apologia — a defense (the technical legal term for a formal defense in a trial; the readiness is for official interrogation). to en hymin elpidi — the hope that is in you (the hope as the distinctive marker that makes the community explicable and questionable).
16: syneīdēsin echontes agathēn — having a good conscience (syneidēsis = consciousness, co-knowing; the good conscience is the internal correlate of the good conduct observable from outside). hina en ō katalaleisthe — so that in the matter in which you are slandered (katalaleō = to speak down against); kataischynthōsin — they might be ashamed; the reversal of shame as in 2:6.
17: ei theloi to thelēma tou Theou paschein — if the will of God wills for you to suffer (the cognate construction theloi to thelēma emphasizes the divine will as the framework; suffering is not God's will in the sense of being preferred, but may be his will in the sense of being permitted and purposed). agathopoiountas ē kakopoiountas — doing good rather than doing evil: the distinction ensures that the suffering in view is specifically unjust suffering of righteous people.
18: Christos hapax peri hamartiōn epathen — Christ once-for-all suffered for sins (hapax = once and once only — the adverb of non-repetition; the once-for-all character distinguishes Christ's death from the repeated Levitical sacrifices). dikaios hyper adikōn — the righteous one for/on behalf of the unrighteous ones (the substitutionary formula: Christ in the place of the unrighteous). hina hymas prosagagē tō Theō — that he might bring you near to God (prosagō = to bring into the divine presence — the language of priestly access). thanatōtheis men sarki, zōopoiētheis de pneumati — put to death in the flesh but made alive in the spirit (the contrasting spheres of death and life).
19: en ō kai tois en phylakē pneumasin poreutheis ekēryxen — in which also, having gone, he proclaimed to the spirits in prison. Three major interpretations: (1) Christ's proclamation of victory to imprisoned fallen angels (Gen 6 background); (2) Christ's pre-incarnate proclamation through Noah; (3) Christ's proclamation to OT dead. The verse identifies the spirits as tois en phylakē (in prison/guardhouse) — a word typically used for divine imprisonment of hostile spiritual beings. The en ō (in which) refers back either to the Spirit or to the state of being made-alive (v.18).
20: apeitheisasin pote — who once disobeyed (aorist participle — a completed historical act); hote apexedecheto hē tou Theou makrothymia en hēmerais Nōe — when God's patience was waiting in the days of Noah (the divine patience as the window of opportunity before judgment). kataskeuazomenēs kibōtou — while the ark was being prepared (present passive — the ongoing construction as the duration of the patience). di'hydatos — through water: the eight were saved through (not from) the water — water as both instrument of judgment and passage to safety.
21: ho kai hymas antitypon nyn sōzei baptisma — which also now saves you as the antitype — baptism. Antitypon is the technical term for the corresponding reality to the type: Noah's flood is the type; baptism is the antitype (the fulfillment that corresponds to the original pattern). ouk sarkos apothesis rhypou — not the removal of bodily filth (baptism is not mere external washing). syneīdēseōs agathēs eperōtēma eis Theon — the pledge/appeal of a good conscience toward God (eperōtēma = a binding legal question/pledge; baptism is an act of commitment/allegiance, not just a washing ceremony).
22: hos estin en dexiā tou Theou — who is at the right hand of God (the post-resurrection exaltation; Ps 110:1 background — the enthroned Lord). hypotagentōn autō angelōn kai exousiōn kai dynameōn — with angels, authorities, and powers subjected to him (aorist passive participle — the subjection is accomplished). The three categories (angels, authorities, powers) are the cosmic hierarchy of spiritual beings; their subjection grounds the believer's confidence in the face of social pressure: the hostile spiritual realities are already under the authority of the risen Christ.
